metadata.title: A Theoretical Analysis of the Test Error of Finite-Rank Kernel Ridge Regression

figures.description: University of Basel Logo and QR Code

sections.title: Contribution
content: 1. Precise non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds for test error;
2. with ridge λ decoupled from sample size N;
3. including inconsistent case (target function outside RKHS) for under-parametrized kernel ridge regression.

sections.title: Kernel Ridge Regression
content: A (finite) rank-M kernel K is a map K: X x X -> R defined for any x, x' in X by K(x, x') = sum_{k=1}^M lambda_k psi_k(x) psi_k(x').
Fix a ridge lambda > 0, the regressor f_z,lambda of the finite-rank kernel K is the (unique) minimizer of the empirical risk functional.

sections.title: Test Error
content: Note that {psi_k}_{k=1}^M are orthonormal functions. Write target function f* = sum_{k=1}^M gamma_k psi_k + f_perp.
The finite-rank KRR test error is decomposed into Bias-Variance: R_z,lambda = bias + variance.
Where bias measures the error when there is no noise in the label, and variance is defined to be the difference between test error and bias.

sections.title: References
content: [1] Mehryar Mohri, Afshin Rostamizadeh, and Ameet Talwalkar. Foundations of machine learning. MIT press (2018).
[2] Arash Amini, Richard Baumgartner, and Dai Feng. Target alignment in truncated kernel ridge regression. NeurIPS (2022).
[3] Francis Bach. Learning theory from first principles. https://www.di.ens.fr/~fbach/ltfp_book.pdf (2023).

tables.description: Comparison table of assumptions and results between this paper and previous works like Mohri, Amini, and Bach

sections.title: Main Result
content: Under mild conditions on the kernel K, for N sufficiently large, there exist constants C1, C2 independent of N and lambda such that with high probability, we have bounds on the test error R_z,lambda.

sections.title: Experiment
content: Our bounds are sharp and capture the decay of the test error in N. The figure below shows the simulation on two different kernels.
We also compare our upper bound with Bach [3]:
